answer:
**Manusmṛti (also known as the *Laws of Manu*), Chapter 7.**

This chapter on the duties of kings personifies *Daṇḍa* (the rod or staff of punishment) as the creation of the primordial Lord (typically Brahmā), explicitly "his own son," embodied as *Dharma* itself from the beginning.[^1] It warns that without the proper application of punishment by the king, "the stronger would roast the weaker like fish on a spit."[^2]

The key verses are *Manusmṛti* 7.14–15 and 7.20 (Kane numbering; other editions may vary slightly):

> [p-73f910] For his sake, the Lord in the beginning created `Daṇḍa`, his own son, as Dharma, the protector of all beings, formed of the spiritual luster of Brahma. // Mn_7.14 //  
> [p-55e886] Through fear of him, all beings, both movable and immovable, submit to their enjoyments and do not swerve from their own Dharma. // Mn_7.15 //[^3]  
>   
> [p-d56424] If the king did not, unwearied, apply punishment to those deserving punishment, the stronger would roast the weaker like fish on a spit. // Mn_7.20 //[^4]

These ideas continue through verses 7.16–28, emphasizing *Daṇḍa* as the guarantor of social order.[^5]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 7*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-73f910).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 7*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-d56424).
[^3]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 7*, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-73f910).
[^4]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 7*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-d56424).
[^5]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 7*, [/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/).

"rod of punishment" OR danda OR "staff of punishment" dharma OR son OR lord
"personifies the rod" OR "punishment as the son"
